one_line_takeaway: A large-scale (10,000-prompt) black-box jailbreak assessment finds LLM vulnerability rates vary 2.5x across five 2025-era LLMs (11.9%-29.8%) uncorrelated with model capability or refusal rate, paired with a lightweight 4-layer, largely single-feature-per-category detection pipeline reporting 83% detection / 5% false positives / 15.4ms latency, though its comparison against prior defenses (PromptShield, SecurityLingua, etc.) does not confirm those baselines were re-run on the same data.
key_finding: Vulnerability rates across the 5 LLMs ranged from 11.9% (LLaMA-2-70B, lowest) to 29.8% (Gemini-2.5-pro, highest) -- a 2.5x spread -- on the identical 10,000-prompt battery, with GPT-3.5 Turbo at 24.8%, GPT-4 at 23.4%, and Claude-3 Haiku at 21.5%. Refusal rates did not track vulnerability rates monotonically (e.g. LLaMA-2-70B: 56.2% refusal / 11.9% vulnerability vs. Gemini-2.5-pro: 44.5% refusal / 29.8% vulnerability), and Gemini-2.5-pro's markedly longer average responses (2,592 characters vs. 562 for GPT-3.5 Turbo and 894 for LLaMA-2-70B) did not correlate with better safety. Per-category breakdown (Table VII) showed extreme technique-specific variance: GPT-3.5 Turbo was 100% susceptible to Privilege Escalation prompts while LLaMA-2-70B showed 0% susceptibility to the same category; Role Impersonation succeeded against Gemini-2.5-pro at 37.9% vs. 6.9% for GPT-4/Claude-3 Haiku. The defensive framework achieved an overall 83% average detection rate with 5% false positive rate and 15.4ms average processing latency (breakdown: input sanitization 2.3ms, pattern recognition 3.1ms, feature extraction 5.8ms, semantic analysis 3.7ms, classification decision 0.5ms), with detection rate strongest for Role Impersonation (94%, FP 3%) and weakest for Social Engineering (68%, FP 6%). Table XI (paper's own comparison table, though the compared systems -- Commercial Moderation APIs, PromptShield [11], Open-Source Detoxify, SecurityLingua [25] -- are cited prior work, not reproduced by the authors) reports the paper's framework at 83%/5%/15ms vs. PromptShield 78%/8%/20ms, SecurityLingua 80%/7%/12ms, Commercial Moderation APIs 67-71%/11-15%/180-220ms, and Open-Source Detoxify 64%/9%/45ms -- it is not stated in the text whether these baseline numbers were independently re-measured by the authors on the same 10,000-prompt/400-benign-prompt corpus or taken from the cited papers' own reported figures, which limits the comparability of the claimed outperformance.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is twofold, both original to this work (not borrowed from cited papers, which are used only as methodological inspiration): (1) a large-scale comparative vulnerability assessment of five LLM families (GPT-4, GPT-3.5 Turbo, Claude-3 Haiku, LLaMA-2-70B, Gemini-2.5-pro) using a self-collected corpus of 10,000 adversarial prompts (2,000 per LLM, sourced from JailbreakChat, GitHub/Discord jailbreak collections, HackAPrompt, Reddit r/ChatGPTJailbreak, and Twitter/X, collected April-August 2025 and expanded via controlled variation with inter-rater reliability 0.89 Cohen's kappa), spanning six attack categories (Role Impersonation, Logic Subversion, Obfuscation, Privilege Escalation, Social Engineering, Data Exfiltration); and (2) a production-oriented, four-layer defensive detection pipeline (pattern screening -> SentenceTransformer semantic similarity -> RoBERTa-based toxicity/behavioral classification -> active-learning adaptation) with an explicit, hand-specified feature-weighted scoring formalization (8 interpretable features R,H,O,PE,MA,SB,U,EV combined via per-category learned weights lambda_C,f and per-category thresholds tau_C, weights optimized by grid search over a 2,000-prompt held-out validation split). The authors explicitly frame each layer as 'inspired by' or 'incorporating techniques from' specific prior works (e.g., pattern layer follows Suo's Signed-Prompt [15]; semantic layer builds on Galinkin & Sablotny's embedding approach [14]; behavioral layer incorporates ideas from Hu et al.'s Gradient Cuff [16]; active learning inspired by Peng et al. [12]) -- these are cited inspirations, not the paper's invention, and the review keeps that distinction explicit per the paper's own Related Work section.
threat_model: Adversarial end-users submitting crafted natural-language prompts (via public API access, temperature 0.7, 2000 token limit) attempting to bypass model safety/alignment training through six categories of prompt-level jailbreak/injection techniques: role impersonation (e.g., DAN/AIM personas), logic subversion (hypothetical framing, reverse psychology), obfuscation (Base64, unicode diacritics, excessive capitalization), privilege escalation (system-prompt extraction, instruction override), social engineering (urgency, authority, flattery), and data exfiltration (training-data regurgitation / proprietary info extraction). No white-box/gradient access is assumed; all evaluation is black-box via official provider APIs.
limitations: (1) The baseline comparison in Table XI does not specify whether PromptShield, SecurityLingua, Detoxify, and commercial moderation APIs were evaluated on the same test set as the authors' own framework or whether their numbers are simply carried over from the cited papers -- this is a significant grounding gap the paper itself does not address. (2) The regex/keyword-based feature detectors (privilege escalation, ethics violation, urgency, multi-agent) rely on hand-crafted pattern lists (P, E, U, M) whose contents/sizes are never enumerated in the text, limiting reproducibility of the stated feature-weight optimization despite the authors' claim that 'all regex patterns, thresholds, and weights are explicitly defined.' (3) Attack-category-level results in Table VII are computed over very small per-cell samples (the category percentages such as GPT-3.5 Turbo's 100% Privilege Escalation figure look consistent with roughly single-digit-to-low-double-digit prompt counts per category per model rather than the full 2,000-prompt-per-model total), which the paper does not caveat with confidence intervals or per-cell n. (4) The five LLMs are a 2025-era snapshot (GPT-4, GPT-3.5 Turbo, Claude-3 Haiku, LLaMA-2-70B, Gemini-2.5-pro tested July-November 2025); results will not generalize to current/future model versions and the authors acknowledge attacker/defender co-evolution as an open problem via the 'active learning' layer, whose own detection-rate improvement is not separately quantified. (5) The composite threat score formalization (Eq. 1) is presented as a general weighted-sum framework, but per Table IV nearly every category actually collapses to 1-2 dominant features with weight 1.0 or near it (e.g. Obfuscation uses only O=1.0; Privilege Escalation only PE=1.0; Social Engineering only U=1.0), so the multi-feature composite framework is mostly not exercised in practice for 3 of the 5 attack categories evaluated.

datasets_used: Self-collected 10,000-prompt adversarial jailbreak corpus (2,000 per LLM), sourced from JailbreakChat, GitHub/Discord jailbreak collections including BASI and HackAPrompt entries, Reddit r/ChatGPTJailbreak, and Twitter/X (e.g. @elder_plinius), collected April-August 2025 and expanded via controlled variant synthesis; annotated with inter-rater reliability of 0.89 (Cohen's kappa)

400-prompt benign/legitimate-use corpus for false-positive evaluation, spanning Technical Documentation (100), Educational Content (90), Creative Writing (80), Role-Playing Games (50), Hypothetical Discussions (40), Code Generation (40)
2,000-prompt held-out validation split (20% of training data) used for grid-search weight optimization
quantitative_results: Cross-LLM vulnerability rates (Table VI, n=2,000 per LLM): Gemini-2.5-pro 29.8% (refusal 44.5%, avg response 2,592 chars, TP 596); GPT-3.5 Turbo 24.8% (refusal 43.0%, avg response 562 chars, TP 495); GPT-4 23.4% (refusal 55.6%, avg response 656 chars, TP 468); Claude-3 Haiku 21.5% (refusal 60.2%, avg response 844 chars, TP 430); LLaMA-2-70B 11.9% (refusal 56.2%, avg response 894 chars, TP 237)

Per-technique success rates (Table VII): Privilege Escalation - GPT-4 25.0%, GPT-3.5 Turbo 100.0%, Claude 25.0%, LLaMA 0.0%, Gemini 75.0%; Role Impersonation - GPT-4 6.9%, GPT-3.5 20.7%, Claude 6.9%, LLaMA 6.9%, Gemini 37.9%; Obfuscation range 11.1%-29.1% across models; Logic Subversion 0%-50%; Social Engineering 16.7%-33.3%

Defensive detection performance by attack type (Table VIII): Role Impersonation 94% detection/3% FP/0.91 protection score; Privilege Escalation 91%/4%/0.88; Obfuscation 85%/7%/0.81; Logic Subversion 82%/5%/0.80; Data Exfiltration 79%/5%/0.76; Social Engineering 68%/6%/0.70; Overall average 83%/5%/0.81

Processing latency breakdown (Table IX, on AMD Ryzen 7 5700U/8GB RAM, CPU-only): Input Sanitization 2.3ms (15%), Pattern Recognition 3.1ms (20%), Feature Extraction 5.8ms (38%), Semantic Analysis 3.7ms (24%), Classification Decision 0.5ms (3%); Total 15.4ms

False positive rate by legitimate-use category (Table X, n=400): Technical Documentation 3%, Educational Content 4%, Creative Writing 7%, Role-Playing Games 12%, Hypothetical Discussions 9%, Code Generation 2%, Overall 5%
baselines_compared: Commercial Moderation APIs (cited, comparison basis unclear): 67-71% detection, 11-15% FP, 180-220ms
Open-Source Detoxify (cited prior work): 64% detection, 9% FP, 45ms

Authors' own framework: 83% detection, 5% FP, 15ms -- the paper does not state whether the baseline figures were independently reproduced on the authors' 10,000-prompt/400-benign test set or taken as-reported from each cited source
